question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: Short answer - Classify this as a 1st‑level support ticket. A very similar incident was resolved by first‑line actions (adjusting Windows camera/app permissions and restarting Teams), without needing deeper engineering
context: SOURCE 42: ﻿ Ticket summary: Ticket name: KF52: Microsoft Teams – Camera not detected Ticket number: TI-1615042 Company: Customer Company 52 AG Contact person: Contact Person 09 Owner: Employee 33 Origin: Email Type: Incident Created on: 07.10.25 09:12 Closure Closure date: 07.10.25 11:06 Closure text: Camera available again in Teams. Root cause was missing operating system permission and blocked device setting. Steps performed according to Blueprint. Activities 07.10.25 09:05 – Email from Contact Person 09 Good morning For one user, the camera is no longer working in Teams. No camera is displayed in the Teams device settings. In other apps it also appears to be partially unavailable. Could you please assist? Kind regards 07.10.25 09:12 Ticket created and assigned to Employee 33. 07.10.25 09:30 – Helpdesk inquiry Good day Could you please confirm whether the user is using Windows or Mac and whether the camera is available in the browser (e.g., Teams Web)? Thank you. 07.10.25 09:48 – Customer response Windows 11. The camera also does not work in the browser. 07.10.25 10:25 – Internal note Connected to the user via remote session. The camera is visible in Device Manager, but Teams has no access. In Windows privacy settings, camera access for apps is disabled. Additionally, Teams is not enabled in the list of allowed apps. According to “Blueprint – Teams Client Devices & Permissions – Version 1.2”, the standard checks were performed (OS privacy settings, app permissions, Teams restart). 07.10.25 10:55 – Customer feedback User confirms that the camera is now displayed in Teams and the test call is successful. 07.10.25 11:06 – Ticket closure Dear Contact Person 09 The camera was blocked by operating system permissions. After adjusting the camera and app permissions according to the Blueprint, the camera is available again in Teams. Kind regards Consulting Sample Company

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . Why (evidence) - In the comparable case, the camera was visible in Device Manager but Teams had no access. The root cause was OS privacy settings (camera access for apps disabled) and Teams not enabled in the allowed apps list. Adjusting those settings per the Teams Client Devices & Permissions blueprint restored the camera in Teams

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?

In the referenced incident these settings were blocking Teams and changing them fixed the issue

question: Is the statement supported by the evidence?
answer: . When to escalate to 2nd‑level - Escalate if the above blueprint checks do not restore camera access (i.e., permissions changed but Teams still shows no camera). - Escalate if you identify driver/agent problems or need to perform system‑level reinstallations or changes to domain/group policies that require elevated privileges or coordination with the OS/Infrastructure team. (A different ticket shows reinstalling an agent resolved a deeper sensor problem, illustrating that some fixes go beyond normal helpdesk changes)
